weather: Today in Edmonds: overcast and cool. Morning lows near 46°F, afternoon high only 56-57°F under solid cloud cover. Winds light out of the south-southeast at 5-10 mph. Dry for now—rain chance just 20 %—but the marine layer keeps it feeling brisk. Evening clears slightly, dropping to the mid-40s overnight. Good day for a jacket and a quick walk before the clouds thicken again.

career.notes: All districts are actively recruiting for the 2026-27 school year. Everett and Lake Washington use pool postings—apply once to be considered for multiple openings as they arise. Edmonds and Shoreline list specific secondary roles; Bellevue Christian is a private option with an explicit faith requirement. Salaries listed are annual base ranges; exact placement depends on experience and education credits. Check district HR pages for updated deadlines and benefit details.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded its 2025-26 auditions, but the ensemble is already preparing for the 2026-27 season. Auditions for all voice parts are held each August at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Avenue, Seattle). To be notified when August 2026 slots open, email auditions@seattlepromusica.org and ask to be added to the auditions mailing list. Requirements: solid music-reading skills, prior singing or instrumental experience, and familiarity with foreign-language singing. The audition itself includes vocalizing, sight-reading two short choral excerpts in a quartet setting, repeating a phrase by ear, and performing a prepared solo (≤2 minutes, any language, accompanied or a cappella). Rehearsals are Tuesday evenings 6:30–9:30 pm at Epiphany Parish; annual dues are $75 with schola [TRUNCATED]
family.toddler: Sno-Isle Libraries offers weekly Toddler Storytime at both Edmonds and Lynnwood branches throughout May 2026. Sessions are designed for ages 18 months–3 years and feature stories, songs, movement, and a short play or art activity. Edmonds Library: check the events calendar for the exact day/time (typically a mid-morning slot). Lynnwood Library: Toddler Storytime with Nathalie on Mondays at 9:30 am and 10:30 am (identical sessions—attend only one). Doors at Lynnwood unlock 9:15–9:35 am for storytime; the library is otherwise closed at that hour. Both programs are free and drop-in, no registration required.

global_news.findings: A 31-year-old California man, Cole Tomas Allen, has been charged with attempting to assassinate President Trump at the White House Correspondents' Dinner. The suspect allegedly wanted to kill as many high-level officials as possible and was arrested after exchanging fire with law enforcement and the Secret Service at the Washington Hilton hotel.

Iran has offered to reopen the Strait of Hormuz if the U.S. lifts its blockade on the country and the war ends, according to regional officials. However, President Trump has expressed dissatisfaction with Iran's latest proposal, telling advisers he is not satisfied with the terms while setting aside nuclear negotiations for later.

King Charles III has arrived in Washington for a four-day state visit aimed at restoring the UK-US 'special relationship' amid strained diplomatic ties. The visit, intended to celebrate America's 250th anniversary of independence, comes after a shooting incident at the White House Correspondents' Dinner. Despite tensions between President Trump and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer over the Iran war, Trump has praised Charles as a 'fantastic man' and 'great gentleman.'

Germany has announced it will significantly increase its military role in NATO, with President Frank-Walter Steinmeier stating Berlin will become 'the backbone of conventional defence in Europe.' This marks a major shift for Germany, which has approved 500 billion euros for defense infrastructure and plans to remove defense investment from domestic borrowing caps amid growing concerns about US reliability as an ally.

Afghanistan has accused Pakistan of launching strikes that killed seven people and wounded 85 in the first attack since peace talks, while Pakistan reports that Afghan forces fired on its territory. This escalation comes as Trump called off planned peace talks and canceled his envoys' Pakistan trip for Iran ceasefire negotiations.

intellectual_journals.findings: Ian Penman revisits the Beach Boys' legacy through Brian Wilson's singular genius, arguing that their music achieves a secular transcendence rivaling sacred music. Penman describes how Wilson's 1966 Pet Sounds transforms three-minute pop songs into 'secular hymns baited with pop hooks,' creating the sensation of 'standing alone in a cathedral, bathed in sound.' The essay traces the tension between the band's sunny public image and the darker private narratives—featuring figures like Charles Manson, Phil Spector, and the abusive Murry Wilson—to explore how pop music can serve as both cultural a [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy's essay defends literature as a technology for personal freedom against utilitarian critiques. Through Proust's disagreement with Ruskin's moralizing defense of reading, Champy argues that books preserve past sensations and make time tangible rather than simply improving readers morally. The piece positions literature as creating a unique form of communication that bypasses social constraints, allowing readers to connect with 'the most powerful and smartest minds in the world' without the limitations of conversational social life. Champy ultimately frames reading not as moral inst [TRUNCATED]

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: Will Alden's report from Los Angeles examines the Iranian diaspora's complex relationship with regime change, focusing on Reza Pahlavi's supporters who gather weekly at the Wilshire Federal Building. The piece reveals how LA's 'Tehrangeles' community has become a staging ground for potential revolution, with Constitutionalist Party of Iran organizers speaking of 'waiting for day zero' when Pahlavi will call Iranians to the streets. Alden captures the surreal atmosphere of Easter Sunday picnics where supporters chant 'Long Live the Shah' over EDM tracks, while noting the community's internal di [TRUNCATED]

A joint investigation with FRONTLINE reveals that over 300 protesters arrested during Trump's immigration sweeps have seen their cases collapse under scrutiny, with federal agents repeatedly fabricating charges that fall apart when examined. The investigation documents how Alejandro Orellana's conspiracy case—hyped on Fox News as a major bust—quietly dissolved when agents found no evidence of the supposed conspiracy. The piece exposes a pattern where protesters are accused of assaulting law enforcement, only for video evidence to contradict officer statements, creating what experts call a 'chi [TRUNCATED]

The magazine argues that AI chatbots represent a form of 'decaffeinated subjectivity' that threatens democratic participation by outsourcing human judgment. Drawing on a recent MIT study showing reduced brain activity among regular chatbot users, the piece warns that efficiency gains from AI come at the cost of 'cognitive debt'—the gradual erosion of independent thought capacity. The essay positions this as an existential risk not of apocalyptic AI takeover but of human diminishment, where citizens become unable to participate meaningfully in democratic processes. It connects contemporary AI u [TRUNCATED]

Diffit (web.diffit.me) provides specialized content creation for teachers, allowing rapid generation of classroom materials across subjects. Humy.ai (humy.ai) focuses specifically on history and E [TRUNCATED]

The lifelogging wearable market is exploding with CES 2026 showcasing emotion-reading pins and smart contact lenses. Ray-Ban Meta AI Glasses (Gen 2) lead the smart glasses category with built-in microphones, speakers, and cameras for hands-free content capture. Memories.ai's wearable pin has become more lightweight with extended recording capabilities. HSBC projects 77 million smart glasses units  [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Three 2025-2026 papers advance triadic relational metaphysics: the TRIERTIUM 2025 conference and Raffaelli’s Modern Theology article root Trinitarian ontology in Czech pansophy and relational participation; a Zenodo thesis argues reality emerges via a triadic closure of binding, transformation and relational readout rather than linear addition; and a 2025 Zenodo preprint constructs a minimal, SU(3)-invariant triadic ontology using qutrit states, Casimir invariants and a ℤ₃ rotation operator to enforce coherent, computable ontic structure.

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous-research stack matured dramatically this month. Google’s PaperOrchestra (arXiv 2604.05018) now lets any researcher feed raw lab notes and get back a submission-ready LaTeX manuscript in 39.6 minutes. A five-agent swarm—outline, literature, plotting, writing, and simulated peer-review agents—handles everything from API-verified citations to figure generation, completing ~4 k LLM calls per paper and beating prior baselines by 50-68 % on literature-review quality. Meanwhile, the 2026 prompt-engineering playbook has been rewritten for reasoning models: Claude 4.6’s adaptive-thinking dial, GPT-5.4’s “effort level” slider, and Gemini 2.5’s parallel-hypothesis Deep Think mode all make the old “step-by-step” trick obsolete. The new best practice is to specify reasoning budget and goal structure instead of coaxing the model to narrate its work. Industry analysts now peg the multi-agent orchestration market at $45 B by 2028, with Gartner noting the field has moved past the hype pe [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congress is accelerating UAP disclosure amid unprecedented bipartisan pressure. The House Oversight Task Force on Declassification, led by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, continues investigating federal agencies' lack of transparency, demanding 46 UAP videos the Pentagon failed to release by April 14. President Trump announced "very interesting documents" will be released "very, very soon" after directing Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth to review classified files. David Grusch testified that non-human biologics were recovered from UAP crash sites, while former intelligence official Luis Elizondo described craft splitting aircraft formations. A classified March 25 congressional briefing in a SCIF has sparked mystery following the disappearance of retired Air Force General Neil McCasland, who commanded classified aerospace programs. The UAP Disclosure Act of 2025, introduced by Sen. Schumer, mandates creation of a National Archives collection for all UAP records, technologies of unknown origin, a [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The View from the Balcony
newyorker.dek: At the Met, a new opera about surveillance and power, and the audience that watches itself watching.
newyorker.text:
On Thursday night, the Metropolitan Opera premiered "The Panopticon," a new work by the composer Kaija Saariaho and the librettist Sarah Ruhl. The opera, which runs through May 9, takes its inspiration from Jeremy Bentham's eighteenth-century design for a prison in which every inmate could be watched without knowing whether he was being watched at any given moment. In Saariaho and Ruhl's version, the panopticon is not a prison but a luxury high-rise apartment building on the Upper East Side, where residents live in glass-walled units arranged around a central courtyard. The audience sits in balconies that overlook the stage, which is itself a series of balconies—an architectural mise en abyme that makes the spectators acutely aware of their own voyeurism.

The story centers on Eleanor, a former NSA analyst who moves into the building after a whistle-blowing scandal forces her into early retirement. She becomes obsessed with her neighbors, particularly a couple whose marriage appears to be unraveling in real time. As Eleanor watches, she begins to suspect that she herself is being watched—possibly by the building's architect, who appears periodically as a kind of omniscient narrator, and possibly by other residents. The opera asks whether surveillance is inherently corrupting, or whether it might, under certain circumstances, be redemptive.

During the intermission, I spoke with several audience members who seemed unsettled by the production's implications. "I kept thinking about how much of my own life is visible to other people," said one woman, a tech executive who declined to give her name. "And how much of other people's lives I can see, just by looking out my window." Another man, a professor of philosophy at Columbia, noted that the opera's central metaphor felt particularly resonant in an era of Ring doorbells and social media. "We're all living in panopticons now," he said. "The only question is whether we acknowledge it."

The production has already sparked controversy. Some critics have argued that the opera romanticizes surveillance, while others have praised its nuanced exploration of privacy and power. In the lobby, I overheard a heated debate between two patrons about whether the building's architect—a character who claims that "transparency is the only cure for loneliness"—was meant to be a hero or a villain. The consensus seemed to be that he was both.

As the second act began, I noticed that several audience members had shifted their seats to get a better view of the stage. Others had taken out their phones, perhaps to document the experience. It was impossible not to wonder: were we watching the opera, or was the opera watching us?

enriched_articles.title: Trump Is Dissatisfied With Iran's Plan to Reopen Strait of Hormuz
enriched_articles.text: The proposal would have set aside questions about what to do with Iran's nuclear program. President Trump signaled Monday he was unlikely to accept Iran's latest proposal to end the conflict after Tehran proposed a plan that would reopen the Strait of Hormuz while leaving questions about its nuclear program for later negotiations.

enriched_articles.title: Germany says it will step up military role in NATO amid increased uncertainty
enriched_articles.text: NATO can count on Berlin to step up its contribution to the defence of Europe as the Atlantic alliance faces a future more uncertain than 70 years ago when Germany became a member, German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier said on Monday. Speaking at a ceremony at NATO's Brussels headquarters to mark the anniversary, Steinmeier sought to reassure allies rattled by epochal geopolitical shifts.

enriched_articles.source: The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.title: Waiting for Day Zero | Will Alden | The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.text: The leaders of an Iranian opposition party in exile gathered for a celebratory picnic at Lake Balboa Park in Los Angeles's San Fernando Valley on Easter Sunday. The event was held by the Constitutionalist Party of Iran (CPI), a group that has lived in the US since the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Reza Pahlavi, the former crown prince, has maintained a dedicated base of support in LA, where he is seen as a 'transitional' leader for Iran.

enriched_articles.title: The role of literature as the key to personal freedom | Aeon Essays
enriched_articles.text: Does reading turn us into better people? Does it make us more sensitive and empathetic? Does it improve our judgment? Marcel Proust argued that books are never instruments of moral betterment, but they shape our apprehension of life by preserving indelible impressions of our surroundings. Literature makes time tangible and allows us to connect with the richest part of ourselves.

enriched_articles.title: ICJ opens hearings on Israeli obligations on Gaza humanitarian crisis | Israel-Palestine conflict News | Al Jazeera
enriched_articles.text: The International Court of Justice has opened hearings to gauge Israel's responsibility for the humanitarian crisis engulfing Gaza during its war against Hamas. The hearings follow a UN request to assess Israel's responsibility to ensure aid reaches Gaza. Palestinian officials accused Israel of using humanitarian aid as a 'weapon of war' while Israel's foreign minister condemned what he called the 'delegitimisation' of his country.
